serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of grilled corn ribs is approximately 150 g, equivalent to roughly 3–4 corn rib pieces cut from one ear of corn.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Corn, sweet, yellow, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (FDC ID 169998) combined with butter (FDC ID 173410) and soy sauce (FDC ID 172238) component estimation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for grilled corn ribs with butter-shoyu glaze. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked sweet corn (FDC ID 169998), unsalted butter (FDC ID 173410), and soy sauce (FDC ID 172238) in proportions consistent with a typical butter-shoyu corn rib preparation (approximately 70% corn, 15% butter, 15% soy sauce by weight contribution to final dish). Input per_100g values from the source record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this USDA component estimate and found broadly consistent. Values retained from input with USDA cross-validation. Singapore HPB Foodo database consulted but no matching entry found for this specific preparation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283. Sweet corn, GI = 52 (glucose reference).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 52 sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweet corn (boiled). Grilled corn ribs with butter-shoyu glaze use sweet corn as the primary carbohydrate source; grilling and butter-shoyu coating are not expected to substantially elevate GI above the boiled corn reference. The added sugar in the shoyu glaze may marginally raise effective GI; however, the fibre content (2.33 g/100g) provides modest buffering. GI estimated at 52 (low category) based on sweet corn analogue. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (52 × 16.67 g carbohydrate × 1.50) / 100 = approximately 13.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 433 mg/100g — exceeds the 400 mg/100g threshold; soy sauce and butter are primary contributors
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g and request low-sodium soy sauce or reduced-glaze preparation to bring sodium intake below 400 mg per 100 g; the saturated fat from butter (6.67 g/100g) also warrants substitution with a small amount of olive oil to reduce combined cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar from shoyu glaze (6.67 g/100g) combined with moderate carbohydrate (16.67 g/100g) and low-to-moderate fibre (2.33 g/100g) raises postprandial glycaemic concern
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving to 100 g or fewer, request the butter-shoyu glaze on the side or ask for a reduced-sugar preparation, and pair with a high-fibre side dish to blunt the postprandial glucose response; the GI of approximately 52 (low) is acceptable but the added sugar load from the glaze requires portion control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.67 g/100g (exceeds 5 g/100g threshold) and trans fat 0.33 g/100g from butter, combined with sodium 433 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this dish in standard portions due to elevated saturated fat (6.67 g/100g) and trans fat (0.33 g/100g) from butter and high sodium (433 mg/100g) from soy sauce; substituting butter with extra-virgin olive oil and using low-sodium soy sauce would substantially reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.67 g/100g from butter exceeds 5 g/100g pro-inflammatory threshold; sodium 433 mg/100g may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute butter with extra-virgin olive oil to reduce saturated fat below 5 g/100g and request reduced soy sauce to lower sodium; corn itself is nightshade-free and provides vitamin C (6.67 mg/100g), but the current preparation negates these anti-inflammatory benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Corn Ribs with Butter Shoyu is a contemporary grilled vegetable snack or side dish originating from Singapore's casual dining scene, popularised by Japanese-influenced fusion restaurants. The dish is prepared by quartering whole ears of sweet corn lengthwise into long, curved 'rib'-shaped pieces, which are then grilled over high heat until the kernels char slightly and the natural sugars caramelise. The corn ribs are finished with a savoury-sweet glaze made from butter and shoyu (Japanese soy sauce), sometimes with a small amount of sugar or mirin, producing a glossy, umami-rich coating. The result is a dish with a satisfying combination of textures — tender, juicy corn kernels with lightly charred edges — and a flavour profile that balances sweetness from the corn and glaze with the deep umami of soy sauce and the richness of butter. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 187 kcal, 3.7 g protein, 12 g fat (of which 6.7 g is saturated fat from butter), 16.7 g carbohydrate, 6.7 g sugar, 2.3 g dietary fibre, and 433 mg sodium. The high sodium content from soy sauce makes this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without modification. The saturated fat from butter exceeds clinical thresholds for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis management. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low category) based on sweet corn analogue data, but the added sugar in the glaze warrants portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. A standard serving is approximately 150 g. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan (contains dairy and soy), and contains soy and dairy allergens. It is not suitable for halal diets without verified halal-certified soy sauce and butter.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for grilled corn ribs with butter-shoyu glaze. Nutrient values were estimated by cross-validating the input ai_generated values (confidence 0.7) against USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked sweet corn, butter, and soy sauce. The component-based estimate is broadly consistent with the input values, supporting medium confidence. GI is estimated from the sweet corn analogue in the University of Sydney GI Database. A high confidence rating is not warranted because no exact dish match exists in any approved primary source.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific grilled corn ribs with butter-shoyu preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-17. 2. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific preparation not available in approved sources; estimated ~270 mg/100g in input health notes but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore set to -1. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite preparation not available in approved sources as of 2026-04-17. 4. Input cultural tags ('Sushi', 'Tempura', 'Grilled Fish') were removed as they are not applicable to this dish. 5. Cuisine reclassified from 'Japanese' to 'Japanese-Singaporean Fusion' as the dish is a Singapore-originated fusion item using Japanese shoyu. 6. Halal status set to false as standard soy sauce and butter may not be halal-certified; consumers should verify with the restaurant. 7. Trans fat (0.33 g/100g) is present from butter; this is naturally occurring trans fat (vaccenic acid / conjugated linoleic acid) rather than industrial trans fat, but is reported as provided in the source record. 8. GI glycemic load calculation: GI 52 × carbohydrate 16.67 g × serving 150 g / 100 = 13.0 g GL per serving. 9. Iteration 3 fix: ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type corrected from 'broader' (not a valid enum value) to 'closest_parent', which is the correct allowed value per the validator schema requiring one of ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Removed inapplicable ethnic tags (Sushi, Tempura, Grilled Fish). Reclassified cuisine from 'Japanese' to 'Japanese-Singaporean Fusion'. Added mandatory multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Japanese. Added GI value from University of Sydney GI Database sweet corn analogue. Added cardiovascular_disease health context. Renamed 'diabetes' condition to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes'. Documented all -1 fields in notes. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases array. Added USDA FoodData Central as approved nutrient source replacing grabfood/foodo.sg.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'analogous_estimate' to 'broader' — however 'broader' was also not a valid enum value per the validator schema.

Iteration 3: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent', which is the correct allowed enum value per validator schema requiring one of ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. G5 hard gate now passes. All six hard gates pass and score remains 8/8.
